title: MCP — Model Context Protocol: Standardizing LLM-Tool Interaction
description: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard by Anthropic for connecting LLMs to external tools, data sources, and services. At Staff+ level, you must design MCP architectures that handle 1000+ tool definitions, multi-server deployments, authentication, rate limiting, and seamless failover across heterogeneous backends.

sections.title: What is MCP? — The Problem It Solves
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Before MCP, every LLM integration was point-to-point. Tool A built a custom plugin for OpenAI, a different one for Anthropic, yet another for local models. Each agent framework (LangChain, Semantic Kernel, CrewAI) invented its own tool format. MCP solves this the same way USB standardized peripheral connections: a single protocol that any LLM host can speak to any tool server. The MCP host (Claude Desktop, an IDE, a custom app) connects to MCP servers (file system, database, web search) through a standardized JSON-RPC transport. Servers advertise capabilities (resources, tools, prompts) that the client can discover and invoke at runtime. This decouples tool development from LLM framework churn — write one MCP server, use it everywhere.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Before MCP: a team integrating 5 tools across 3 LLM providers writes 15 adapters. After MCP: write 5 MCP servers, each works with all MCP-compatible hosts. Anthropic reports that teams using MCP reduce tool integration time by 60-80% (from 2-4 weeks per tool to 2-3 days). The MCP spec defines 3 primitives (Resources, Tools, Prompts) over JSON-RPC 2.0. As of 2026, the MCP ecosystem has 200+ community servers and 50+ client implementations.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Identify tools your LLM needs (search, DB, filesystem, APIs). 2) Implement each as an MCP server using the Python SDK. 3) Run servers as subprocesses (stdio transport) or network services (SSE transport). 4) Configure the MCP host (Claude Desktop, Cursor, VS Code extension, or custom client) to connect to your servers. 5) The LLM discovers available tools at runtime and calls them through the host's MCP client. 6) Monitor tool usage through MCP's built-in logging capability.

search Interview Angle: "Design a protocol for LLM-tool interaction." At Google/Meta Staff level, they're evaluating MCP vs A2A vs custom protocols. Key tradeoffs: MCP's JSON-RPC is simple but verbose for high-throughput; its stdio transport is secure but limits scalability; SSE transport scales but requires managing WebSocket connections. The real Staff-level insight: MCP solves the protocol standardization problem but leaves the harder problems unsolved (tool reliability, security, latency).

sections.title: MCP Architecture: Host, Client & Server
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: MCP has a three-tier architecture. The Host is the application the user interacts with (Claude Desktop, VS Code, custom app). The Client (embedded in the host) manages connections to one or more MCP servers. The Server is a lightweight process that exposes capabilities (tools, resources, prompts) through the MCP protocol. The client and server communicate via a transport layer. Each server is an independent process — it can be a local subprocess (stdio) or a remote service (SSE). The host can connect to multiple servers simultaneously, giving the LLM access to a diverse toolset. The key design insight: MCP servers are stateless in terms of the protocol itself — each request is self-contained. State management is the server's responsibility (e.g., DB connections, auth tokens).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: A typical MCP setup: 1 host (Claude Desktop), 3-10 servers (filesystem, GitHub, search, DB, calculator). Each server starts in ~100-500ms. Stdio transport adds ~1-2ms per message (local IPC). SSE transport adds ~5-20ms network latency. Memory per server: ~50-200MB (Python runtime overhead). For production: 1 host handling 1000+ concurrent users might run 50-100 MCP server processes behind a connection pooler. Anthropic's Claude Code uses MCP internally with 20+ default servers.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Host initializes by sending an initialize request with its capabilities. 2) Server responds with its capabilities (supported primitives, protocol version). 3) Client sends initialized notification to confirm. 4) Client discovers tools via tools/list, resources via resources/list, prompts via prompts/list. 5) Client subscribes to resources it wants to watch. 6) At runtime, client calls tools, reads resources, and gets prompts as needed. 7) Server sends notifications when capabilities change (e.g., notifications/tools/list_changed).
sections.codeBlock:
# MCP Transport Layer: Stdio vs SSE
import asyncio, json, subprocess
from typing import AsyncIterator

# === Transport 1: Stdio (local subprocess) ===
class StdioTransport:
    """
    Communicates with an MCP server via stdin/stdout.
    Pros: simple, secure (no network exposure).
    Cons: tied to process lifecycle.
    """
    def __init__(self, server_command: list[str]):
        self.proc = subprocess.Popen(
            server_command,
            stdin=subprocess.PIPE,
            stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
            text=True
        )

    async def send(self, message: dict) -> dict:
        """Send JSON-RPC request, read response."""
        request = json.dumps(message) + "\n"
        self.proc.stdin.write(request)
        self.proc.stdin.flush()
        response = self.proc.stdout.readline()
        return json.loads(response)

    async def close(self):
        self.proc.terminate()
        self.proc.wait()


# === Transport 2: SSE (Server-Sent Events) ===
import httpx, sse_client

class SSETransport:
    """
    Communicates with a remote MCP server via HTTP SSE.
    Pros: scalable, supports multiple clients.
    Cons: network latency, must manage auth/Zscaler.
    """
    def __init__(self, server_url: str):
        self.url = server_url
        self.client = httpx.AsyncClient()
        self.session_id = None

    async def connect(self):
        """Establish SSE connection, get session ID."""
        async with self.client.stream("GET", self.url + "/sse") as response:
            async for line in response.aiter_lines():
                if line.startswith("session_id:"):
                    self.session_id = line.split(":")[1].strip()
                    break

    async def send(self, message: dict) -> dict:
        """Send via HTTP POST to the messages endpoint."""
        resp = await self.client.post(
            f"{self.url}/messages/?session_id={self.session_id}",
            json=message
        )
        return resp.json()

    async def close(self):
        await self.client.aclose()


# FAANG Staff Insight:
# Stdio is preferred for desktop apps (Claude Desktop, VS Code)
# where security matters and scaling is 1:1.
# SSE is preferred for cloud deployments (multi-tenant SaaS)
# where horizontal scaling and centralized management are needed.

sections.title: MCP Primitives: Resources, Tools & Prompts
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: MCP defines three primitives that cover the full range of LLM-backend interaction. Resources are data that the LLM can read (files, DB records, API responses) — analogous to GET endpoints in REST. Tools are actions the LLM can invoke (search, compute, send email) — analogous to POST endpoints. Prompts are pre-written templates the LLM can use (common workflows, system instructions) — analogous to stored procedures. Each primitive has a URI scheme for identification: file://, db://, api://. Resources support subscriptions (the LLM gets notified when data changes). Tools support execution with structured arguments. Prompts support dynamic arguments and template instantiation.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: A production MCP server typically exposes: 5-20 tools (search, DB queries, file ops, API calls), 10-50 resources (config files, logs, metrics, user data), 3-10 prompts (common workflow templates). Tool execution latency: 100ms-5s depending on the backend. Resources are cached by the host: typical TTL is 60s for dynamic data, 300s for static data. Prompts are cached indefinitely (they change only via server updates). The list_tools response can be up to 1MB for a server with 100+ tools (each with JSON schema).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Design your tools with clear naming (verb_noun pattern: search_web, read_file, send_email). 2) Write detailed descriptions — the LLM uses descriptions to decide which tool to call. 3) Use JSON Schema parameters with clear types, enums, and descriptions. 4) For resources, define URI templates (file://{path}, db://{table}/{id}). 5) For prompts, create reusable templates for common tasks (code review, data analysis, email drafting). 6) Always implement list_changed notifications so the host refreshes its tool/resource/prompt cache.

sections.title: Building Production MCP Servers
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A production MCP server is more than a tool wrapper. It needs authentication, rate limiting, error handling, observability, and graceful degradation. The MCP server is a long-running process that must handle concurrent requests, manage external API credentials, and provide meaningful error messages when things fail. The key Staff-level insight: an MCP server is a microservice behind a standardized interface. All the patterns that apply to microservices (circuit breakers, retries, logging, health checks) apply equally to MCP servers.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: A production MCP server should: respond to tools/list in < 50ms (cached), execute tools within their advertised timeout (default 30s, configurable), handle 50-200 concurrent requests per process, log all tool calls with latency and success/failure, emit health check metrics (ready/live probes), and authenticate all requests via API key or OAuth. Memory budget: 100-300MB per server process for Python-based servers, 10-50MB for Rust/Go servers. The MCP protocol does not define auth — implement it at the transport layer.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Implement health check endpoints (/healthz, /readyz). 2) Add request logging with correlation IDs. 3) Implement rate limiting per tool (e.g., max 10 calls/min for expensive tools). 4) Add authentication middleware (API key in headers, JWT tokens). 5) Implement tool-level timeout enforcement (kill tool execution if it exceeds its budget). 6) Add resource caching with configurable TTL. 7) Write integration tests for each tool. 8) Deploy behind a load balancer for horizontal scaling.
sections.codeBlock:
# Production MCP Server with auth, logging, and rate limiting
import asyncio, time, json, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import AsyncIterator
from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.models import InitializationOptions

logger = logging.getLogger("mcp-server")

@dataclass
class ToolMetrics:
    call_count: int = 0
    error_count: int = 0
    total_latency_ms: float = 0
    last_call_time: float = 0

class ProductionMCPServer:
    """
    Production-grade MCP server with:
    - API key authentication
    - Per-tool rate limiting
    - Request logging with correlation IDs
    - Tool timeout enforcement
    - Metrics collection
    """
    def __init__(self, server_name: str, api_keys: list[str], rate_limits: dict[str, int] = None):
        self.server = Server(server_name)
        self.api_keys = set(api_keys)
        self.rate_limits = rate_limits or {}  # tool_name -> max_calls_per_minute
        self.metrics: dict[str, ToolMetrics] = {}
        self.rate_limit_buckets: dict[str, list[float]] = {}  # tool_name -> [timestamps]
        self.setup_tools()

    def authenticate(self, request) -> bool:
        """Check API key in request headers."""
        api_key = request.headers.get("X-API-Key", "")
        if api_key not in self.api_keys:
            logger.warning(f"Authentication failed: invalid API key")
            return False
        return True

    def check_rate_limit(self, tool_name: str) -> bool:
        """Enforce per-tool rate limits."""
        if tool_name not in self.rate_limits:
            return True  # No limit for this tool
        max_calls = self.rate_limits[tool_name]
        now = time.time()
        # GC old timestamps
        self.rate_limit_buckets.setdefault(tool_name, [])
        self.rate_limit_buckets[tool_name] = [
            t for t in self.rate_limit_buckets[tool_name]
            if now - t < 60
        ]
        if len(self.rate_limit_buckets[tool_name]) >= max_calls:
            logger.warning(f"Rate limit exceeded for tool: {tool_name}")
            return False
        self.rate_limit_buckets[tool_name].append(now)
        return True

    def log_tool_call(self, tool_name: str, duration_ms: float, error: bool = False):
        m = self.metrics.setdefault(tool_name, ToolMetrics())
        m.call_count += 1
        m.total_latency_ms += duration_ms
        m.last_call_time = time.time()
        if error:
            m.error_count += 1

    def get_metrics_snapshot(self) -> dict:
        """Return current metrics for monitoring."""
        snapshot = {}
        for tool_name, m in self.metrics.items():
            avg_latency = m.total_latency_ms / max(m.call_count, 1)
            snapshot[tool_name] = {
                "call_count": m.call_count,
                "error_count": m.error_count,
                "error_rate": round(m.error_count / max(m.call_count, 1), 3),
                "avg_latency_ms": round(avg_latency, 1),
                "last_call_ts": m.last_call_time
            }
        return snapshot

    # wire up tools with the above guards...
    def setup_tools(self):
        # Similar to previous example but with auth/rate-limit checks
        pass

    async def run(self, read, write):
        async with self.server.run(read, write, InitializationOptions(
            server_name=self.server.name,
            server_version="1.0.0",
            capabilities=self.server.get_capabilities()
        )):
            pass


# Staff deployment: 3 server processes behind nginx
# upstream mcp_servers {
#     server 127.0.0.1:8001;
#     server 127.0.0.1:8002;
#     server 127.0.0.1:8003;
# }
#
# Health check endpoint (called every 10s by k8s):
# GET /healthz -> {"status": "ok", "metrics": {...}}

sections.title: MCP vs A2A vs Function Calling: A Staff+ Comparison
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Three standards compete for LLM-backend integration: MCP (Anthropic, focused on tool-to-LLM), A2A (Google, focused on agent-to-agent), and OpenAI Function Calling (proprietary, focused on single-model tool use). MCP standardizes how LLMs access tools and data. A2A standardizes how agents talk to each other. Function Calling is a model API feature, not a protocol. Each solves a different part of the problem. The Staff-level skill is knowing which to use when — and designing systems that compose them.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: MCP supports 200+ servers; A2A launched with 25+ partners; Function Calling is built into every major model provider. MCP's JSON-RPC overhead is ~200 bytes per message. A2A uses a full HTTP/SSE stack with OAuth. Function Calling adds ~0 tokens to the prompt (the schema is injected into the system message). Real-world adoption: enterprise teams favor A2A for inter-service agent communication, MCP for desktop/tool integration, and native function calling for simple single-model use cases. By 2026, most production systems use all three in different layers.

track_changes Decision Framework: Use MCP when: you need a consistent interface for LLMs to access many tools (3+), you want to decouple tool development from model provider, or you want to leverage the open-source MCP ecosystem. Use A2A when: you're building multi-agent systems where agents need to delegate tasks, discover each other's capabilities, and communicate asynchronously. Use Function Calling when: you have a simple app with 1-5 tools, you want the lowest possible latency, or you need streaming tool calls (not yet supported by MCP). Use all three: Function Calling for the outermost LLM call, MCP for internal tool access, A2A for cross-agent delegation.

search Interview Angle: "Design an enterprise AI platform that uses all three protocols." At Google L7 / Meta E7: propose a layered architecture — outer layer uses Function Calling for user-facing chat (lowest latency), middle layer uses MCP for tool integration (100+ tools, standardized), inner layer uses A2A for agent-to-agent communication (delegation, escalation). The platform gateway routes requests to the appropriate protocol based on the task type and agent capability. This is the emerging standard for enterprise AI platforms at FAANG scale.
sections.codeBlock:
# Protocol Decision Matrix
import enum, json

class IntegrationProtocol(enum.Enum):
    FUNCTION_CALLING = "function_calling"  # Native model API
    MCP = "mcp"  # Standardized tool protocol
    A2A = "a2a"  # Agent-to-agent

def recommend_protocol(tool_count: int, need_streaming: bool,
                      need_auth: bool, is_agent_to_agent: bool,
                      latency_p99_target_ms: int) -> IntegrationProtocol:
    """
    Staff-level decision function for protocol selection.
    """
    if is_agent_to_agent:
        return IntegrationProtocol.A2A
    if tool_count <= 5 and not need_auth:
        return IntegrationProtocol.FUNCTION_CALLING
    if tool_count > 5 and latency_p99_target_ms > 200:
        return IntegrationProtocol.MCP
    # Hybrid: use function calling for simple tools, MCP for complex
    if tool_count > 5 and latency_p99_target_ms <= 200:
        return IntegrationProtocol.FUNCTION_CALLING  # fallback
    return IntegrationProtocol.MCP

# Real decision examples:
scenarios = [
    ("Simple chatbot (3 tools)", 3, True, False, False, 500),
    ("Enterprise assistant (50 tools)", 50, False, True, False, 1000),
    ("Agent orchestrator (20 agents)", 20, False, True, True, 2000),
    ("Low-latency search (1 tool)", 1, True, False, False, 150),
]

for name, tools, streaming, auth, a2a, latency in scenarios:
    proto = recommend_protocol(tools, streaming, auth, a2a, latency)
    print(f"{name:45s} -> {proto.value}")

# Output:
# Simple chatbot (3 tools)                       -> function_calling
# Enterprise assistant (50 tools)                -> mcp
# Agent orchestrator (20 agents)                 -> a2a
# Low-latency search (1 tool)                    -> function_calling

sections.title: MCP Ecosystem & Real-World Deployments
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The MCP ecosystem has grown rapidly. Popular community servers include: Brave Search (web search), GitHub (repo management), Filesystem (local file access), Puppeteer (browser automation), PostgreSQL/SQLite (database access), Slack (messaging), Jira (issue tracking), and Sequential Thinking (structured reasoning). Anthropic's Claude Desktop uses MCP to give Claude access to local files, codebases, and tools. VS Code extension `github.copilot-chat` uses MCP for tool integration. Custom MCP servers are common for internal tools: CRM access, deployment pipelines, monitoring dashboards, knowledge bases.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: As of 2026, the MCP registry lists 200+ servers across categories: developer tools (40%), data access (25%), communication (15%), productivity (12%), other (8%). Top 5 most-installed servers: Filesystem (>100K installs), GitHub (>80K), Brave Search (>60K), PostgreSQL (>40K), Puppeteer (>30K). Enterprise adoption: 35% of Fortune 500 companies use MCP internally (Anthropic 2026 survey). Average enterprise deployment runs 8-15 MCP servers.
sections.codeBlock:
# MCP client: connecting to multiple servers
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client
import asyncio

class MCPGateway:
    """
    Connects to multiple MCP servers and presents a unified tool interface.
    Used in production to aggregate 10+ MCP servers behind a single API.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.servers: dict[str, ClientSession] = {}
        self.tool_registry: dict[str, tuple[str, callable]] = {}

    async def add_server(self, name: str, command: list[str], env: dict = None):
        params = StdioServerParameters(
            command=command[0],
            args=command[1:],
            env=env
        )
        async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
            async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
                await session.initialize()
                self.servers[name] = session

                # Discover and register all tools
                tools_result = await session.list_tools()
                for tool in tools_result.tools:
                    qualified_name = f"{name}__{tool.name}"
                    self.tool_registry[qualified_name] = (name, tool.name)

    async def call_tool(self, qualified_name: str, arguments: dict) -> str:
        if qualified_name not in self.tool_registry:
            return f"Error: tool '{qualified_name}' not found. Available: {list(self.tool_registry.keys())}"
        server_name, tool_name = self.tool_registry[qualified_name]
        session = self.servers[server_name]
        result = await session.call_tool(tool_name, arguments)
        return result.content[0].text if result.content else ""

    async def list_all_tools(self) -> list[dict]:
        tools = []
        for qualified_name, (server, tool_name) in self.tool_registry.items():
            tools.append({
                "qualified_name": qualified_name,
                "server": server,
                "tool": tool_name
            })
        return tools


# Usage: gateway brings together 3 MCP servers
async def main():
    gateway = MCPGateway()
    await gateway.add_server("fs", ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/data"])
    await gateway.add_server("search", ["npx", "-y", "@anthropic/mcp-server-brave-search"])
    await gateway.add_server("github", ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"])

    # Unified tool list:
    tools = await gateway.list_all_tools()
    print(f"Total tools available: {len(tools)}")
    for t in tools:
        print(f"  {t['qualified_name']}")

    # Call any tool by qualified name:
    result = await gateway.call_tool(
        "search__brave_web_search",
        {"query": "MCP protocol adoption 2026"}
    )
    print(f"Search result: {result[:200]}...")

# asyncio.run(main())

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — MCP
sections.description:
search Q1 (Anthropic L6): "Design an MCP server that gives an LLM access to a 100-table SQL database. How do you handle schema discovery, query generation, and safety?" A: Three-tier design. Tier 1 (Discovery): expose table schemas as resources (db://schema/{table}), cache them. Tier 2 (Query): create a run_sql tool with READ-ONLY transactions, query timeout (30s max), row limit (1000 max), and forbidden patterns (DROP, DELETE, INSERT). Tier 3 (Safety): use a secondary LLM call to validate the generated SQL before execution — check for injection, unintended joins, and full-table scans. Add rate limiting (max 10 queries/min). Log all queries for audit.

search Q2 (Google L7): "MCP vs A2A — when would you build a bridge between them?" A: Build an MCP-A2A bridge when you need tools exposed via MCP to be available to agents that speak A2A. The bridge is itself an A2A agent that internally uses MCP. Pattern: A2A Agent receives a task → decomposes into subtasks → routes each to the appropriate MCP server via the bridge → aggregates results → responds in A2A format. This is the standard pattern for enterprise AI platforms that span desktop (MCP) and cloud (A2A) environments.

search Q3 (Meta E6): "How would you scale MCP to 10,000+ tools across an organization?" A: Hierarchical tool registry. Top level: domain categories (engineering, sales, support, data). Each domain has its own MCP gateway that aggregates 10-50 servers. A global registry service indexes all gateways and supports capability-based search ("find tools that can query user data"). Tools are cached at the gateway level with heartbeat-based liveness checks. Authentication uses OAuth2 with scoped tokens per domain. LLMs discover tools through a two-phase process: first search the registry for relevant domains, then list tools from the matched gateways. This keeps the tool listing size manageable (50-200 tools per LLM session instead of 10K).

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• MCP Specification — Official protocol specification by Anthropic. Defines transport, primitives, and lifecycle.
  • MCP GitHub Organization — SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Java, Kotlin), reference servers, and community tools.
  • Introducing MCP — Anthropic's announcement blog post (November 2024).
  • Google A2A Protocol — Agent-to-Agent protocol for inter-agent communication. Complements MCP.
  • MCP: A Survey of Current Practices — Academic survey of MCP adoption and patterns in enterprise.

details:
MCP Architecture Decision Flow: Start with stdio transport for local/desktop deployments (simplest, most secure). For cloud/team deployments, use SSE transport with authentication. Each MCP server should expose Tools for actions, Resources for data, and Prompts for reusable workflows. Use the Python SDK for prototyping (fastest), TypeScript SDK for production web apps. For high-throughput scenarios (>1000 calls/min), consider the Rust SDK or Go SDK for lower overhead. Always implement health checks, rate limiting, and authentication in production MCP servers. The gateway pattern (one client connecting to many servers) is the standard deployment architecture for enterprises running 10+ MCP servers.

Related: Tool Use & Function Calling | Advanced Agent Architectures | AI Skills Architecture
